questions.question_text: Newton's first law of motion is best described by which of the following statements about a body and its state of rest or motion?

options.text: A body remains at rest or in uniform motion unless an unbalanced force acts on it
Force is always equal to mass divided by acceleration
Every action has an equal and opposite reaction force
Bodies in motion eventually stop without any external cause

questions.explanation: Newton's first law, the law of inertia, states that a body at rest stays at rest and a body in uniform motion continues in a straight line unless acted upon by an unbalanced external force, explaining why athletes must apply force to accelerate.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Force = mass × acceleration is the second law expression.
Action–reaction is described by Newton's third law.
Bodies stop only because of opposing forces like friction.

questions.question_text: Newton's second law of motion in basic algebraic form is most accurately written as which of the following equations of mechanics?

options.text: F = m × a
F = m / a
F = a / m
F = m + a

questions.explanation: Newton's second law states that the net force F on a body equals its mass m multiplied by the acceleration a it experiences (F = m × a); a heavier body needs more force to obtain the same acceleration as a lighter one.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Mass divided by acceleration is dimensionally inconsistent with force.
Acceleration divided by mass does not yield force.
Adding mass and acceleration is dimensionally meaningless.

questions.question_text: Which of the following sporting situations best illustrates Newton's third law of motion in the action of a swimmer pushing off from the pool wall?

options.text: Swimmer pushes wall backwards; wall pushes swimmer forwards equally
Swimmer cancels the wall's resistance entirely
Swimmer's force on the wall is much larger than wall's reaction
Swimmer experiences no force from the wall at all

questions.explanation: Newton's third law: for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction; the swimmer pushes the wall backwards, and the wall exerts an equal and opposite forward force on the swimmer, providing the propulsive thrust off the pool wall.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The wall's reaction is what propels the swimmer forward.
Action and reaction forces are always equal in magnitude.
A wall in contact always exerts a reaction force on the swimmer.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the static friction acting between an athlete's foot and the ground when the athlete is just about to start running?

options.text: Friction that acts only when the foot is sliding on the ground
Friction that resists motion when the foot is still stationary on the ground
Friction that exists only inside the muscles of the leg
Friction that acts at right angles to the running direction

questions.explanation: Static friction is the force that opposes the tendency of motion between two surfaces in contact when they are not yet moving relative to each other; it allows the foot to grip the ground at the start of a sprint and provides the push-off force.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Friction acting during sliding is sliding friction.
Friction is between surfaces, not internal to muscles.
Friction acts along the contact surfaces, opposing motion.

questions.question_text: Which of the following types of friction is generally lowest in magnitude for the same pair of surfaces under similar conditions in sports situations?

options.text: Static friction (limiting)
Sliding (kinetic) friction
Rolling friction of a ball on grass
Fluid friction in a vacuum chamber

questions.explanation: For the same pair of surfaces, rolling friction is generally less than sliding friction, which in turn is less than the limiting static friction; this is why a rolling ball slows down much more gradually than a sliding object on the same surface.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Limiting static friction is the largest of the three types.
Sliding friction lies between static and rolling values.
Fluid friction in a vacuum is essentially zero by definition.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why studded football boots are worn instead of smooth-soled shoes on natural grass surfaces during a competitive match?

options.text: To reduce friction so that players slip more during play
To increase friction with the surface for better grip and control
To make the boots heavier and slow the player down
Solely to comply with cosmetic fashion preferences

questions.explanation: Studs penetrate the grass surface and increase the effective friction between boot and ground; this enhanced grip improves acceleration, deceleration and quick changes of direction, all crucial in football, while reducing slips on wet or soft turf.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Reducing friction would worsen, not improve, performance.
Stud weight is negligible for performance impact.
Functional design dominates over fashion considerations.

questions.question_text: Which of the following pairs of factors most directly influences the magnitude of friction between two solid surfaces in contact during sports activity?

options.text: Nature of surfaces in contact and the normal reaction force
Atmospheric pressure and the colour of the surfaces
Time of day and ambient temperature only
Number of spectators and presence of music nearby

questions.explanation: Friction depends mainly on the nature of the contacting surfaces (rough, smooth, hard, soft) and the normal reaction force pressing them together; for ordinary surfaces, friction is approximately the coefficient of friction multiplied by the normal force.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Atmospheric pressure has negligible effect on solid friction.
Time of day does not directly change friction values.
Audience and music do not affect mechanical friction.

questions.question_text: A heavy shot-put implement is harder to start moving than a light tennis ball under the same applied force; which of Newton's laws best explains this observation?

options.text: Newton's first law alone
Newton's second law (F = ma)
Newton's third law alone
None of Newton's laws explain this observation

questions.explanation: By Newton's second law a = F / m, so for the same force a heavier mass produces a smaller acceleration; the shot's larger mass therefore experiences much less acceleration than the lighter tennis ball under identical applied force.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: First law explains rest or uniform motion, not differing accelerations.
Third law concerns equal and opposite reactions.
Newton's laws clearly do explain everyday motion observations.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why a sudden braking car causes its passengers to lurch forward in their seats during the deceleration phase?

options.text: Application of Newton's third law to the seats
Inertia of the passengers as described by Newton's first law
Increased gravitational force during deceleration
Sudden disappearance of the seat belt's tension

questions.explanation: By Newton's first law, the passengers' bodies tend to continue in uniform forward motion; when the car decelerates, only the car body slows initially, so the passengers lurch forward relative to the car until the seatbelt or seat applies a force on them.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Third law concerns equal-opposite forces, not lurching.
Gravity does not change during routine deceleration.
Seat belt tension does not 'disappear' during braking.

questions.question_text: A long-jump pit is filled with soft sand chiefly because of which of the following biomechanical reasons related to Newton's second law and impulse?

options.text: Soft sand reduces the time of impact, increasing landing force
Soft sand prolongs the time of impact, reducing the peak force on the body
Soft sand reduces gravitational pull during the landing phase
Soft sand makes the jumper bounce upward and add distance

questions.explanation: Impulse equals force multiplied by time; soft sand prolongs the deceleration time of the jumper after landing, so for the same change in momentum the peak force on the body is reduced, lowering injury risk to bones and joints.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Reducing impact time would increase peak force, not decrease it.
Gravitational pull is unchanged by surface softness.
A landing pit is designed to absorb energy, not bounce the jumper.

questions.question_text: Match each of Newton's laws of motion in Column A with the most appropriate sporting example listed in Column B and choose the correct mapping shown in the options.

column_b.text: Heavier shot needs more force for same acceleration
Cricket ball stays at rest until bowler bowls it
Swimmer pushes wall back; wall pushes swimmer forward
Total momentum before and after a collision is preserved

questions.explanation: First law — cricket ball stays at rest until pushed; second law — heavier shot needs more force for the same acceleration; third law — swimmer pushes wall and wall pushes swimmer back; momentum conservation governs collisions in cue sports and contact sports.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: First law is exemplified by inertia (rest until pushed).
Second law links force, mass and acceleration directly.
Third law is the equal and opposite reaction principle.

questions.question_text: Match each type of friction in Column A with its correct definition or example listed in Column B and choose the option showing the right combination.

column_b.text: A football rolling slowly across the grass
Maximum friction before any motion begins
A swimmer moving through water in the pool
A cricket bat dragged across the wooden floor

questions.explanation: Limiting static friction is the maximum frictional force just before motion begins; sliding friction acts during dragging like a bat on a floor; rolling friction acts on a rolling ball; fluid friction acts on a swimmer moving through water.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Static friction maxes out just before motion starts.
Sliding friction acts during continuous dragging.
Rolling friction is associated with rotating round objects.

questions.question_text: Match each sport application of friction in Column A with the most appropriate biomechanical purpose listed in Column B and choose the correct option for the combination.

column_a.text: Studded boots on natural grass field

column_b.text: Increase grip and friction with the ground
Reduce friction so skaters glide easily
Increase friction to grip apparatus securely

questions.explanation: Studs increase grip with ground; smooth ice reduces friction so skaters glide; resin powder increases friction so gymnasts grip the apparatus; streamlined swimsuits reduce fluid friction in water — distinct sport applications using or reducing friction.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Studs increase friction; ice reduces friction.
Resin is used to increase grip on apparatus.
Streamlined gear reduces fluid resistance in water.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason about Newton's third law in sport and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in athletic mechanics.
questions.assertion: A sprinter runs faster on the track when their feet exert a stronger backward push on the ground at each step.
questions.reason: By Newton's third law, the ground exerts an equal and opposite forward reaction force on the sprinter's foot during contact.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: By Newton's third law, the ground reacts with an equal and opposite forward force on the sprinter's foot during contact; a stronger backward push therefore produces a stronger forward reaction, accelerating the sprinter and explaining the assertion.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason gives the correct mechanism for the assertion.
Both statements align with sprinting biomechanics evidence.
Action-reaction directly explains forward propulsion.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about friction in sport and choose the option that best represents their logical connection in athletic equipment design.
questions.assertion: Bowling a cricket ball on a polished smooth pitch makes the ball slide further than on a rough dusty pitch.
questions.reason: A smoother surface generally produces a lower coefficient of sliding friction between the ball and the pitch.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: A smoother surface lowers the coefficient of sliding friction between the ball and pitch; with less friction the ball loses kinetic energy more slowly and slides further before stopping or rolling, directly explaining the assertion's observation.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason correctly identifies the friction mechanism.
Both statements are mechanically accurate.
Lower friction directly produces greater slide distance.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason regarding inertia in sport and choose the option that correctly describes their logical relationship in mechanical analysis.
questions.assertion: A heavy shot-putter sliding into the front of the throwing circle continues to drift forward even after the implement is released.
questions.reason: By Newton's first law, the shot-putter's body tends to maintain its existing motion until an unbalanced external force such as friction stops it.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: By Newton's first law of inertia, the shot-putter's body continues in motion after release until an unbalanced external force — chiefly friction with the circle and the foul-line board — decelerates it, directly explaining the forward drift after release.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason gives the correct first-law explanation.
Both statements are physically consistent.
Inertia clearly drives the post-release drift.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following events in the chronological order in which Newton's three laws are illustrated when a sprinter takes off from the starting blocks of a 100-metre race.

items.text: Sprinter applies force to push off the starting blocks
Sprinter remains stationary in set position before the start
Block applies an equal and opposite reaction on the sprinter
Sprinter accelerates forward according to F = ma

options.text: Q → P → R → S
P → Q → R → S
S → R → P → Q
R → P → S → Q

questions.explanation: First, the sprinter is at rest by inertia (first law); on the gun, they apply a force to the blocks; the blocks apply an equal and opposite reaction (third law); the resulting net force accelerates them forward according to F = ma (second law).
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Inertia precedes the application of any force.
Action force precedes the reaction force.
Acceleration follows the resulting net force.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following frictional events in the correct chronological order as observed when a footballer kicks a stationary ball that rolls and finally stops on the grass.

items.text: Static friction between ball and ground holds it in place
Foot strike imparts force greater than friction's resistance
Rolling friction gradually slows the ball on grass
Ball comes to a complete stop on the field surface

options.text: P → Q → R → S
Q → P → S → R
R → S → P → Q
S → R → Q → P

questions.explanation: Initially static friction holds the ball at rest; the foot strike supplies a force exceeding limiting static friction, setting it rolling; rolling friction then gradually decelerates the ball; once kinetic energy is lost, the ball comes to a complete stop.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Static friction acts on the ball at the start.
Rolling friction follows the kicking action.
The complete stop is always the final event.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: For the same pair of surfaces, sliding friction is generally less than the limiting static friction during normal sports activity.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Sliding (kinetic) friction is typically less than the limiting static friction for the same pair of surfaces; this is why it generally takes a slightly larger force to start moving an object than to keep it moving once it is sliding.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Limiting static friction must be overcome to start motion.
Once sliding, friction drops slightly to kinetic value.
This is a standard result in classical mechanics.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram comparing Newton's three laws of motion with their sports applications, and identify the law that is best illustrated by a swimmer pushing the wall to start a lap.

options.text: Newton's first law of inertia
Newton's second law (F = m × a)
Newton's third law of action-reaction
Law of conservation of energy alone

questions.explanation: A swimmer pushing the wall during a tumble turn illustrates Newton's third law: the wall exerts an equal and opposite reaction on the swimmer's feet, propelling them forward through the water at the start of the next lap of the race.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: First law is best shown by a stationary athlete at rest.
Second law involves comparing different masses or forces.
Conservation of energy is a separate principle, not Newton's laws.
